content.problemStatement:
Alice has an array AAA consisting of NNN **distinct** integers. Bob takes exactly N−1N - 1N−1 elements from this array and adds a positive integer XXX (i.e. X>0X \gt 0X>0) to each of these numbers and then shuffles them to form a new array BBB of length N−1N - 1N−1.

You are given both arrays AAA and BBB. You have to identify the value of XXX chosen by Bob. If there are multiple possible values of XXX, print the **smallest** of them. It is guaranteed that for the given input, there exists at least one possible value of XXX.

**Note:** Since the input is large, prefer using fast input methods.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
Each test case contains 333 lines of input.
The first line contains an integer NNN - the length of array AAA.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​, denoting the array AAA.
The third line contains N−1N - 1N−1 space-separated integers B1,B2,…,BN−1B_1, B_2, \dots, B_{N-1}B1​,B2​,…,BN−1​, denoting the array BBB.


Output Format
For each test case, output the value of XXX chosen by Bob. In case there are multiple possible values of XXX, print the smallest of them.
constraints: 2 ≤ N ≤ 10^5 (sum of N over all test cases ≤ 5·10^5)
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains N (2 ≤ N ≤ 10^5). The second line contains N distinct integers A1…AN. The third line contains N‑1 distinct integers B1…B(N‑1).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with the smallest possible positive integer X that could have been added by Bob.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: remove 3, X=7 gives B={8,11,15}. Test case 2: remove 8, X=2 gives B={10}. Test case 3: remove 4, X=1 gives B={3}.

solution.approach: For each test case sort A and B. The unknown removed element can be either the first element of the sorted A or some later element. Consequently only two candidates for X need to be examined: 1) X = B[0]‑A[0] (assume the removed element is not A[0]), 2) X = B[0]‑A[1] (assume A[0] is removed). For each candidate X>0 we verify using two‑pointer scanning: walk through A and B, allowing at most one skip in A. If a_i+X equals b_j we advance both pointers; otherwise we skip a_i (only once). If we finish scanning successfully, X is feasible. Among feasible candidates output the smallest X. Sorting dominates the complexity, O(N log N) per test case, O(N) extra memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case (sorting), O(N) for verification.
